title: Trust in Autonomous Human-Robot Collaboration: Effects of Responsive Interaction Policies
text:
arXiv


Trust plays a central role in human–robot collaboration, yet its formation is rarely examined under the constraints of fully autonomous interaction. This pilot study investigated how interaction policy influences trust during in-person collaboration with a social robot operating without Wizard-of-Oz control or scripted repair. Participants completed a multi-stage collaborative task with a mobile robot that autonomously managed spoken-language dialogue, affect inference, and task progression. Two interaction policies were compared: a responsive policy, in which the robot proactively adapted its dialogue and assistance based on inferred interaction state, and a neutral, reactive policy, in which the robot provided only direct, task-relevant responses when prompted. Responsive interaction was associated with significantly higher post-interaction trust under viable communication conditions, despite no reliable differences in overall task accuracy. Sensitivity analyses indicated that affective and experiential components of trust were more sensitive to communication breakdown than evaluative judgments of reliability, and that as language-mediated interaction degraded, the trust advantage associated with responsiveness attenuated and ratings became less clearly interpretable as calibrated evaluations of collaborative competence. These findings suggest that trust in autonomous human–robot interaction emerges from process-level interaction dynamics and operates within constraints imposed by communication viability, highlighting the importance of evaluating trust under real autonomy conditions when designing interactive robotic systems.



Misty-II

title: Shauna Heron
text:
Lab
  
  
      arXiv
  
  
    
     E-mail
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
      ORCID
  

  
  
I am a graduate student and healthcare-focused data scientist and R specialist with hands-on experience building production analytics systems for a mental health organization. I have deep expertise in wrangling large, complex electronic health record datasets, developing predictive models, and delivering reproducible reporting workflows that drive clinical decision-making. Graduate training in experimental psychology and machine learning provides both the statistical depth and the domain literacy to work effectively at the intersection of data science and clinical practice.


Primary: R (tidyverse, tidymodels, Quarto, ggplot2, lme4)
Secondary: Python (scikit-learn, Pandas, PyTorch, TensorFlow, Hugging Face, spaCy, Ollama, LangChain)
Statistics & Modelling: Generalized linear and mixed models; Bayesian hierarchical models; supervised and unsupervised machine learning; SHAP-based model interpretability
Data: Electronic health record (EHR) analytics; longitudinal data construction; feature engineering; cohort definition; SQL/SQLite
Reporting & Workflow: Quarto; Shiny; reproducible PDF and HTML reports; Power BI
Applied AI: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG); LLM integration; vector databases (ChromaDB); Robotics platforms
Tools: Positron, VS Code, Git, NeoVIM, R Studio, Jupyter
OS: Linux/Unix, macOS, Windows

title: Publications
Publications
Publications
Shauna Heron
text:
Compass Child & Youth Mental Health — Sudbury, ON | 2023–Present
Sole data analyst for a 100+ staff mental health agency serving thousands of clients annually, with programs spanning counselling and therapy, walk-in services, and supervised access.

Lead all analytical work on a longitudinal EHR dataset spanning 2013 to present and covering thousands of clients per year — including data extraction, cleaning, linkage, and feature engineering.
Collaborate with clinical teams and management to translate complex analytical outputs into accessible, actionable insights.
Design and maintain reproducible Quarto-based reporting workflows producing weekly, monthly, and quarterly operational and clinical reports used daily by leadership and program managers.
Deliver ad hoc analysis and data briefings directly to clinical and executive leadership to support service planning, resource allocation, and performance improvement.
Researched and implemented two validated organizational health instruments for ongoing (yearly) monitoring of workplace culture and performance. Yearly results are analyzed and compared to prior years; findings and recommendations are presented to organizational leadership.

The SCORE Survey (Safety, Communication, Operational Reliability, and Engagement) designed to evaluate work environments in healthcare, providing actionable insights for clinical teams on topics like teamwork, safety climate, burnout, and emotional exhaustion.

Supervisory Relationship Questionnaire (SRQ) examining the quality of supervisory relationships between clinicians and their supervisors;


Built interpretable machine learning models using R to forecast clinician workload from EHR data, with planned integration into an operational dashboard system.
Designed and built an internal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to enable intelligent querying of the agency’s policy documentation, reducing time-to-answer for staff policy questions.

title: Shauna Heron
text:
Laurentian University — Sudbury, ON | 2023–2026 (expected)

Supervisor: Dr. Michael Emond | Co-supervisor: Dr. Passi, Bharti School of Engineering(Pending defense)

Developed longitudinal feature-engineering pipeline from large-scale EHR data to model client-related clinician workload, directly parallel to production work at Compass.
Built and evaluated interpretable ML models using SHAP-based explanation, with a focus on fairness and operational validity for clinical service settings.

Supervisor: Dr. M. C. Lau, Laurentian Intelligent Mobile Robotics Lab

Designed and implemented a fully-autonomous affect-adaptive HRI system on the Misty-II robotic platform; conducted experimental pilot study on trust and dialogue policy in human–robot collaboration.
Published preprint (arXiv:2603.00154); paper under peer review.

Laurentian University — 2023–2025

Supported undergraduate instruction in Research Methods, Statistics, and Scientific Analysis.
Designed and facilitated a class research project introducing students to reproducible data workflows in R.

title: About Shauna Heron
text: Hi! I am an experimental psychology grad student with hands-on experience building production analytics systems for a large mental health organization. I have deep expertise in wrangling large, complex electronic health record datasets with R and Python; developing predictive models and delivering reproducible reporting workflows that support clinical decision-making. Graduate training in experimental psychology and machine learning provides both the statistical depth and the domain literacy to work effectively at the intersection of data science and clinical practice.
